summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch from NSIS to Inno Setup for his software installer, arguing that visual appearance matters because the installer is the user's first impression. He draws an analogy to his stock icons making software feel 'bright and happy' and notes that in consumer software, looks sell. He acknowledges the tradeoff that Inno Setup requires two extra clicks but plans to optimize that.
key_points: The installer is the first impression customers get of your software and deserves serious attention
In the consumer market, visual polish directly impacts sales — looks sell software
Inno Setup looks more professional than NSIS, justifying the switch despite added clicks
Every additional mouseclick forced on a customer is friction worth eliminating
money_quotes: I haven't really spent all that much time on my installer, which is somewhat silly because its the first look people get of my software and I should really want to nail it.
Especially in the consumer market, looks sell software.
Additional mouseclicks are also something I really hate forcing on my customer.
key_insight: Your installer is your software's first impression — visual polish matters as much there as anywhere else in the product.
